learning_objectives: Analyze the current global game market across PC, console, and mobile segments
Interpret market data from SteamSpy, Newzoo, and Sensor Tower reports
Identify genre trends, market saturation levels, and emerging opportunities
Evaluate the commercial viability of a game concept using market data
readings: The Game Marketing Handbook, Ch. 1-3: Understanding the Marketplace
Newzoo Global Games Market Report 2024 (executive summary)
Simon Carless, 'How That Game Sold on Steam' retrospective series

lecture_notes.introduction: Game marketing begins not with a trailer or a press release, but with a rigorous understanding of the market you are entering. The global games industry generates over $190 billion annually, but that revenue is distributed unevenly across platforms, genres, and business models. A horror roguelike faces a fundamentally different competitive landscape than a cozy farming sim, and the marketing strategies for each must reflect those differences. This session establishes the analytical foundation you will use throughout the course. We will examine how to read market data, identify trends before they peak, and assess whether a game concept has a viable commercial niche. The goal is not to chase trends blindly, but to understand the competitive terrain so you can position your game intelligently. Marketing that ignores market reality is just noise.
key_concepts: Global market segmentation: PC ($40B), console ($55B), mobile ($90B) with distinct audience behaviors and discovery mechanisms in each segment
Steam market dynamics: over 14,000 games released per year, median lifetime revenue under $5,000, with the top 1% capturing the majority of total revenue
Genre market analysis using tag data: identifying oversaturated genres vs. underserved niches by comparing release volume against revenue distribution
Platform audience demographics: age, spending habits, discovery preferences, and content expectations differ significantly between Steam, PlayStation, Xbox, Nintendo, iOS, and Android
Market timing and release windows: avoiding major AAA launches, leveraging seasonal sales events, and understanding the Steam algorithm's new release boost
Competitive analysis methodology: identifying your game's five closest competitors and analyzing their marketing, pricing, review scores, and estimated sales
The concept of a 'comparables analysis' borrowed from film marketing: positioning your game as 'X meets Y' using successful reference points
Revenue estimation techniques: the Boxleiter method, review-to-sales ratios, and Steam wishlist conversion benchmarks
Market validation before production: using Steam tag searches, Reddit community sizes, and Twitch viewership to gauge audience demand
The danger of survivorship bias in market analysis: failed games are invisible, so studying only successes creates a distorted picture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live market analysis of three game concepts using SteamDB, SteamSpy, and the Steam tag system. We will walk through a complete competitive analysis for a hypothetical indie action-roguelike, estimating market size, identifying the top 10 competitors, analyzing their review volumes and estimated revenue, and determining whether the concept has sufficient market space. Students will see how to use the 'Similar Games' feature, tag filtering, and release date analysis to build a data-driven market picture.
setup_requirements: SteamDB and SteamSpy bookmarked and accessible
Spreadsheet software for market data collection and analysis
Sensor Tower free account for mobile market data
Access to Newzoo report summaries through university library

review.value: A large genre market means more potential customers but also more competition. The ideal position is a growing niche with high demand relative to supply, identified by comparing tag popularity against release volume.

Steam revenue follows a power law: the median game earns under $5K lifetime, while the top 1% earns over $1M. Marketing is the primary differentiator between games of similar quality that land in different tiers.

Estimated gross revenue equals number of reviews multiplied by a factor between 20 and 60, depending on genre and price point. This provides a rough but useful revenue estimate for competitor analysis.
On Steam, wishlists convert to purchases at roughly 15-20% on launch day and 40-60% over the first year. A game needs approximately 7,000-10,000 wishlists at launch to have a strong first week.
For every Hollow Knight or Stardew Valley, thousands of games in the same genre sold under 100 copies. Market analysis must account for the full distribution, not just visible successes.

learning_objectives: Define target audience personas using demographic, psychographic, and behavioral data
Develop a positioning statement that differentiates a game in its competitive landscape
Apply the concept of audience segmentation to tailor messaging for different player types
Create audience-specific value propositions that resonate with core and secondary audiences
readings: The Game Marketing Handbook, Ch. 4-5: Knowing Your Player
Quantic Foundry: Gamer Motivation Profile research papers
Bartle's Taxonomy of Player Types (original paper and modern critiques)

lecture_notes.introduction: The most common failure in game marketing is trying to market to everyone. When you market to everyone, you reach no one effectively. Positioning is the art of defining exactly who your game is for and crafting a message that speaks directly to that audience. This session covers audience research methodologies, persona development, and positioning strategy. We will use Quantic Foundry's motivation model, which identifies twelve core gaming motivations grouped into six clusters, to build data-driven player personas. The output is not a vague sense of 'who might like this game' but a specific, actionable audience profile that drives every subsequent marketing decision from trailer editing to influencer selection. A game positioned as 'a challenging Metroidvania for fans of Hollow Knight and Salt and Sanctuary' makes fundamentally different marketing decisions than the same game positioned as 'an atmospheric exploration adventure for fans of Ori and Journey.' Both positions could be valid, but each implies a different audience, different messaging, different influencers, and different community spaces.
key_concepts: Player motivation profiles using Quantic Foundry's twelve motivations: Destruction, Excitement, Competition, Community, Challenge, Strategy, Completion, Power, Fantasy, Story, Design, Discovery
Demographic vs. psychographic segmentation: demographics describe who players are, psychographics describe why they play and what they value
Primary, secondary, and tertiary audience tiers: your core audience buys on day one, your secondary audience buys during sales, your tertiary audience stumbles in through recommendations

Positioning statement formula: 'For [target audience] who want [core desire], [Game Title] is a [genre] that delivers [unique value proposition], unlike [primary competitor] which [key differentiator]'
The concept of 'market position' as a place in the player's mental landscape occupied by your game relative to competitors
Audience validation through community research: Reddit subscriber counts, Discord server activity, Twitch category viewership, and Steam curator followings as proxies for audience size
Messaging hierarchy: tagline (5 words), elevator pitch (30 seconds), description (200 words), and detailed feature list, each targeting different audience attention spans
The danger of feature-centric vs. benefit-centric messaging: players care about experiences, not feature lists
Cultural and regional audience considerations: humor, art style preferences, and genre expectations vary significantly by market
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop exercise: students receive a game concept brief and build a complete audience profile. Starting with the game's core mechanics and themes, we identify the Quantic Foundry motivations it serves, research the size and activity of relevant communities, draft three audience personas with names and behavioral profiles, and write a positioning statement. The class then critiques each positioning statement for clarity and differentiation.
setup_requirements: Quantic Foundry Gamer Motivation Profile survey (take before class)
Persona template worksheet (provided on LMS)
Access to Reddit, Discord, and Twitch for audience research

review.value: A clear positioning statement answers three questions: who is this for, what do they get, and why is this different? If you cannot answer all three concisely, your positioning is not sharp enough.

Two 25-year-old gamers might both play RPGs but for completely different reasons: one seeks challenge mastery, the other seeks narrative immersion. Marketing must speak to motivation, not demographics alone.

Core audience: buys at full price on launch. Secondary: buys during first sale at 20-30% off. Tertiary: discovers through word-of-mouth or deep discounts. Each tier requires different messaging and timing.
'Procedurally generated dungeons' is a feature. 'Every run feels fresh and unpredictable' is a benefit. Marketing copy should lead with benefits and support with features.

learning_objectives: Develop a cohesive brand identity for a game including visual, tonal, and thematic elements
Create brand guidelines that maintain consistency across all marketing touchpoints
Design logos, key art, and visual assets that communicate genre and tone at a glance
Apply color theory and typography principles to game branding
readings: Designing Brand Identity by Alina Wheeler, Ch. 1-4 (adapted for game context)
GDC Talk: 'The Art of the Steam Capsule' by Chris Zukowski
Case studies: brand evolution of Hades, Among Us, and Fortnite

lecture_notes.introduction: Your game's brand is not just a logo. It is the total impression a player has of your game before they ever play it, formed through every visual, verbal, and interactive touchpoint they encounter. In a market where thousands of games compete for attention, brand clarity is survival. A player scrolling through Steam sees your capsule image for approximately 0.3 seconds before deciding whether to click or scroll past. In that fraction of a second, your brand must communicate genre, tone, and quality. This session covers the principles of game branding from the ground up: how to define your brand's personality, translate it into visual and verbal language, and maintain consistency across store pages, social media, trailers, and press materials. We will study games that built iconic brands on shoestring budgets and analyze why some beautifully crafted games fail to establish brand recognition. The common thread in successful game brands is not budget but clarity of vision and relentless consistency in execution.
key_concepts: Brand identity components: name, logo, color palette, typography, visual style, tone of voice, and the emotional promise made to players
The Steam capsule image as the most important single marketing asset: it must communicate genre, quality, and uniqueness at 231x87 pixels
Color theory for game branding: how color palettes signal genre (dark red/black for horror, bright pastels for cozy, neon for cyberpunk) and create instant recognition
Typography as brand signal: font choices communicate era, tone, and genre before a single word is read
Logo design principles for games: readability at small sizes, distinctiveness in a grid of thumbnails, scalability from favicon to banner
Brand voice and tone: defining how your game 'speaks' on social media, in descriptions, and in community interactions
Consistency across touchpoints: the store page, social media, press kit, trailer, and in-game UI should feel like they belong to the same brand
Brand guidelines document: a living reference that ensures every team member and contractor maintains brand consistency
Common branding mistakes: generic fantasy logos, illegible stylized text, key art that does not represent actual gameplay, inconsistent visual language across platforms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live brand development exercise: starting with a game concept, we develop a complete brand identity in class. We select a color palette using Adobe Color and game reference images, choose typography using Google Fonts, sketch logo concepts, and create a mockup Steam capsule image in Figma. The result is a brand guidelines one-pager that could be handed to any artist or marketer on the team.
setup_requirements: Figma or Canva account for brand asset creation
Adobe Color (free online tool) for palette generation
Google Fonts for typography exploration
Steam store page examples bookmarked for reference

review.value: The Steam capsule image is seen by more people than any other marketing asset. It must be readable at thumbnail size, communicate genre instantly, and stand out in a grid of competitors.
Every touchpoint reinforces or dilutes your brand. A Discord server with a different color scheme than your store page creates subconscious dissonance that erodes player trust.

Players have strong genre-color associations built from thousands of games. Using warm earthy tones for a horror game or dark gritty palettes for a cozy game creates cognitive friction that hurts conversion.
Shrink your logo to 32x32 pixels. If it is not recognizable and distinct, it fails the most common real-world use case: browser tabs, Discord icons, and social media avatars.
At minimum: color codes (hex, RGB), approved fonts with sizes, logo usage rules with clear space requirements, tone-of-voice examples, and a library of approved visual assets.

learning_objectives: Build a professional press kit using presskit() or equivalent tools
Write compelling game descriptions optimized for press, store pages, and social sharing
Capture and curate screenshots that tell a story and sell the game experience
Prepare media assets in the correct formats and resolutions for all major platforms
readings: Rami Ismail, presskit() documentation and best practices
GDC Vault: 'Pitching Your Game to the Press' by Tom Francis
Chris Zukowski, 'How to Write a Game Description That Actually Sells'

lecture_notes.introduction: A press kit is your game's resume. When a journalist, streamer, or curator encounters your game for the first time, the press kit is what they use to decide whether it is worth five minutes of their attention, let alone coverage. A well-structured press kit reduces friction between your game and coverage. A poorly organized one, or the absence of one entirely, is an immediate red flag that tells media professionals you are not serious. The modern press kit has evolved far beyond a zip file of screenshots. It is a curated, web-accessible package that tells a complete story: what the game is, who made it, why it matters, and everything a content creator needs to produce coverage without emailing you for assets. This session covers every component of a professional press kit and the craft behind each element. We will examine how top indie studios structure their press materials and why certain description formulas, screenshot compositions, and factsheet formats consistently outperform others in generating coverage.
key_concepts: Press kit components: factsheet, description (short and long versions), screenshot gallery (8-12 images), logo pack (PNG with transparency + vector), key art (multiple resolutions), trailer embed, team bios, contact information, and social links
The presskit() standard by Rami Ismail: a free tool that generates a consistent, journalist-friendly press page hosted on your website
Screenshot composition rules: show gameplay not menus, capture moments of action or beauty, include UI only when it is a selling point, maintain consistent resolution and aspect ratio

Description writing formula: hook sentence (genre + unique twist), core gameplay loop in one paragraph, three to five unique selling points as bullet points, comparison hooks for genre context, and a call to action
Factsheet essentials: developer name, location, release date or window, platforms, price, press contact email, and social links in a scannable format
Key art requirements: multiple aspect ratios (16:9, 1:1, vertical) for different platform needs, must work at both hero banner and thumbnail sizes
Organizing assets for download: separate folders for screenshots, logos, and key art, with a naming convention and resolution guide
Press kit hosting: your own domain is ideal (press.yourgame.com), with fast load times and direct download links for all assets
Updating the press kit: it should evolve as development progresses, with dated entries showing the game's growth trajectory
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on presskit() setup: students install presskit() on a local server, populate it with sample game assets, write the factsheet and descriptions, and publish a live press page. We walk through each field, discussing why certain wording and image choices generate more coverage than others. Students then peer-review each other's press kits using a journalist evaluation rubric.
setup_requirements: presskit() template downloaded from GitHub
Local web server (XAMPP, MAMP, or Python SimpleHTTPServer)
Image editing software for screenshot cropping and key art creation
Sample game project with at least 8 gameplay screenshots

review.value: Every piece of information a journalist has to email you for is a coverage opportunity lost. A complete press kit means a journalist can write about your game in 20 minutes without contacting you at all.

Choose screenshots that tell a story arc: an establishing shot of the world, a moment of core gameplay, a dramatic encounter, a unique mechanic in action, and a visually stunning environment. Avoid redundancy.
Lead with genre and unique twist: 'A turn-based tactics game where you rewind time to undo mistakes.' Follow with gameplay loop, USPs, and comparison hooks. End with platform and date.
Use descriptive file names: GameTitle_Screenshot_CombatSystem_1920x1080.png is useful to a journalist. IMG_0047.png is not. Include resolution in the filename.

learning_objectives: Plan and storyboard a game trailer that communicates genre, tone, and unique selling points within 60-90 seconds
Capture high-quality gameplay footage using screen recording and in-engine camera tools
Edit trailers with professional pacing, music selection, and text overlays
Optimize trailers for different platforms: Steam, YouTube, Twitter/X, and TikTok
readings: Derek Lieu, 'How to Make a Game Trailer' masterclass notes
GDC Vault: 'Trailer Made: What Makes a Great Game Trailer' panel
Analysis of ten trailers that generated over 1 million views on YouTube

lecture_notes.introduction: The game trailer is the single most impactful marketing asset you will create. It is the one piece of content most likely to be shared, embedded in articles, featured on store pages, and watched by potential buyers. A great trailer can carry a game launch; a poor one can doom a good game to obscurity. Trailer production is a specialized craft that borrows from film editing, advertising, and game design simultaneously. The best game trailers are not just montages of gameplay footage set to music. They are carefully structured narratives that answer the viewer's question: 'What is this game and why should I care?' within the first three seconds and then build excitement systematically through pacing, reveals, and emotional escalation. This session covers the complete trailer production pipeline from concept through final export. We will study the anatomy of trailers that generated massive wishlist spikes, break down their editing techniques, and apply those techniques to your own footage. The tools are accessible and largely free, meaning budget is not a barrier to producing a professional trailer.
key_concepts: The three-second rule: viewers decide whether to keep watching within three seconds, so the trailer must hook immediately with a striking visual, bold statement, or intriguing moment
Trailer structure types: the narrative arc (setup, escalation, climax), the feature showcase (mechanic montage), and the tone piece (atmosphere and mood over mechanics)
Storyboarding for game trailers: planning shots before recording, identifying key moments that showcase unique features, and designing the emotional rhythm
Gameplay capture techniques: using in-engine camera tools for cinematic angles, hiding UI elements for clean footage, orchestrating AI behavior for dynamic captures
Music licensing and selection: the right track elevates a trailer from amateur to professional; sources include Artlist, Epidemic Sound, and royalty-free libraries
Pacing and editing rhythm: cutting on the beat, varying shot length to control tension, and using breathing room between high-intensity sequences
Text and typography in trailers: when to use title cards, how to write concise feature callouts, and matching font style to game brand
Platform-specific optimization: Steam autoplay requirements (no sound), YouTube thumbnail selection, Twitter/X 2:20 limit, TikTok vertical format with different pacing expectations
The reveal trailer vs. launch trailer vs. accolades trailer: each serves a different purpose and targets a different audience state
Common trailer mistakes: starting with a logo, showing too many features without focus, using non-gameplay footage without disclosure, and poor audio mixing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Full trailer production walkthrough using DaVinci Resolve (free). Starting with raw gameplay footage from a sample project, we storyboard a 60-second trailer, select and license a music track, capture additional footage with in-engine camera tools, edit the trailer with cuts on beats, add minimal text overlays, and export for Steam and YouTube. Students see every step from raw footage to finished product.
setup_requirements: DaVinci Resolve installed (free version)
OBS Studio for gameplay capture
Sample gameplay footage provided on course portal
Headphones for audio editing work

review.value: The first three seconds determine whether a viewer watches or clicks away. Open with your most visually striking or emotionally compelling moment, not a studio logo or slow fade-in.
Music provides the emotional framework that footage fills in. Choose the track first, then edit footage to match its rhythm and emotional arc. The trailer should feel incomplete without the music.

Gameplay footage is more convincing than text callouts. If a feature can be shown in action, show it. Use text only for information that cannot be communicated visually: title, platforms, and release date.
Steam autoplays trailers muted: the first 5 seconds must work without audio. YouTube favors 60-90 seconds. TikTok and Instagram Reels need vertical 9:16 cuts under 60 seconds with faster pacing.
Announce trailer builds awareness (show the concept). Gameplay trailer builds wishlists (show the loop). Launch trailer converts purchases (show reviews, accolades, and polish).

learning_objectives: Develop platform-specific social media strategies for Twitter/X, TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube
Create a content calendar that maintains consistent audience engagement throughout development
Analyze social media metrics to optimize content strategy based on engagement data
Produce shareable content formats including devlogs, GIFs, short-form video, and behind-the-scenes posts
readings: Chris Zukowski, 'How to Market a Game' blog series on social media
GDC Vault: 'Growing Your Indie Game Community on Social Media'
Case study: Balatro's viral TikTok marketing campaign

lecture_notes.introduction: Social media is the most accessible and potentially the most powerful marketing channel available to game developers, but it is also the most misunderstood. Many developers treat social media as a broadcast channel, posting development updates into the void and wondering why nobody engages. Effective game social media marketing is not broadcasting; it is community building through consistent, valuable, and shareable content. Each platform has its own culture, algorithm, and content expectations. A post that thrives on Twitter/X will fail on TikTok and vice versa. This session covers platform-specific strategies, content creation workflows that are sustainable for small teams, and the metrics that actually predict marketing success. We will examine developers who built massive followings with zero marketing budget and reverse-engineer their content strategies. The common thread is not luck but a systematic approach to creating content that serves the audience's interests while building awareness for the game.
key_concepts: Platform selection strategy: focus on two platforms maximum based on where your target audience spends time, not where you personally prefer to post
Twitter/X for games: short punchy updates, GIF-friendly, strong hashtag discovery (#screenshotsaturday, #indiedev, #gamedev), real-time engagement with community
TikTok for games: short-form video with hook in first second, trending audio integration, behind-the-scenes development content performs well, algorithm favors new creators
YouTube for games: devlog format with personality-driven narration, longer shelf life than other platforms, SEO-friendly for discovery months or years after posting
Content pillars: define 3-5 recurring content types (development updates, behind-the-scenes, community spotlights, educational content, entertainment) and rotate between them
Content calendar creation: planning 2-4 weeks ahead with a mix of content types, tied to development milestones but not dependent on them
Engagement metrics that matter: saves and shares indicate value (people bookmark what they find useful and share what they find remarkable), while likes and follows are vanity metrics
The 80/20 rule for social media: 80% of posts should provide value (entertainment, education, inspiration), 20% can be direct promotion (wishlist calls, announcements)
Batch content creation: recording multiple pieces of content in a single session to maintain consistency without daily production pressure
Handling negative feedback and controversy on public platforms: responding professionally, knowing when to engage vs. ignore, and protecting team mental health
lecture_notes.demonstration: Content creation workshop: students produce three pieces of platform-specific content from a single development session. We capture a 30-second development moment, then craft it into a Twitter/X GIF with caption, a TikTok with trending audio, and a YouTube Shorts clip with voiceover. Students see how one source of raw material becomes three distinct pieces of content optimized for different platforms.
setup_requirements: Social media accounts on at least two platforms
ScreenToGif or ShareX for GIF capture
CapCut or similar for short-form video editing
Content calendar template (Google Sheets or Notion)

review.value: Two platforms done well outperforms five platforms done poorly. Choose based on where your audience already exists, invest in understanding that platform's algorithm and culture.
Content gets shared when it makes the sharer look good: knowledgeable, tasteful, or connected. Create content people want to share because it reflects well on them.
Define 3-5 recurring content types and rotate between them. This prevents creative burnout and ensures a balanced feed that serves different audience needs.
Algorithm weight across platforms increasingly favors saves and shares over likes. Content that people save to reference later signals higher value to the algorithm.

learning_objectives: Design and configure a Discord server optimized for game community engagement
Develop moderation policies and automated tools for sustainable community management
Build a presence on Reddit through genuine participation and strategic posting
Create community feedback loops that inform development decisions
readings: Victoria Tran, 'Community Management in Indie Games' (collected talks and articles)
Discord Community Building Guide (official documentation)
Case studies: Larian Studios, Coffee Stain, and Innersloth community strategies

lecture_notes.introduction: A dedicated community is the most valuable marketing asset a game can have. Communities generate word-of-mouth, provide feedback during development, create user-generated content, defend the game during controversies, and sustain engagement long after launch. But communities are not audiences; they are relationships. Building a community requires genuine investment of time, attention, and vulnerability. You must be present, responsive, and willing to share both successes and struggles. This session covers the practical mechanics of building game communities on Discord and Reddit, the two most important platforms for PC game communities. We will examine server architecture, moderation strategy, engagement mechanics, and the critical transition from a development community to a player community at launch. The key insight is that community building must begin early in development, long before you have a product to sell, because communities take months or years to cultivate and cannot be manufactured on demand.
key_concepts: Discord server architecture: welcome flow, role assignment, channel organization (announcements, general, feedback, fan-art, bugs, off-topic), and the importance of a clear first impression
Moderation strategy: establishing rules early, training volunteer moderators, using bots (MEE6, Carl-bot) for automated moderation, and handling toxicity before it poisons the culture
Engagement mechanics: regular developer updates, community events, polls on development decisions, screenshot contests, and beta testing access as a community reward
Reddit strategy: genuine participation in relevant subreddits (r/gaming, genre-specific subs, r/indiegaming) long before self-promotion, following the 9:1 engagement-to-promotion ratio
Community feedback integration: structured feedback channels, voting systems for feature priorities, and transparent communication about which feedback influenced decisions
The welcome flow: the first 30 seconds in your Discord server determine whether a new member stays or leaves. An automated welcome message, clear rules, and an immediate engagement hook are essential
Scaling community management: what works for 100 members does not work for 10,000. Plan for growth with moderation tiers, FAQ channels, and community-led subgroups
Community metrics: member growth rate, daily active members, message volume, and sentiment analysis as health indicators
Converting community members to customers: the community must never feel transactional, but every engaged member should have a clear path to wishlisting and purchasing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live Discord server setup: we build a game community server from scratch, configuring roles, permissions, welcome flow, and moderation bots. Students set up automated welcome messages, create structured feedback channels, and design a community event. We then analyze three established game Discord servers, noting what works and what creates friction for new members.
setup_requirements: Discord account with server creation enabled
MEE6 or Carl-bot for server automation
Reddit account aged at least 30 days for community participation
Spreadsheet for community metrics tracking

review.value: An audience consumes content passively. A community creates, shares, and advocates actively. Building a community requires giving members agency, recognition, and genuine influence over the game.
A new member's first 30 seconds in your Discord determine retention. An automated welcome, clear navigation, and an obvious first action (introduce yourself, pick a role) are essential.
Reddit communities detect and punish self-promotion. Build genuine reputation through helpful comments and contributions before ever mentioning your game. The 9:1 ratio is a minimum.
Structure feedback channels with clear categories (bugs, suggestions, balance). Share a monthly summary of community feedback that influenced development decisions to reinforce that input matters.

learning_objectives: Identify and prioritize content creators whose audiences align with your game's target market
Write effective outreach emails that get opened and generate coverage
Manage key distribution, embargo timing, and exclusive access strategically
Measure influencer marketing ROI using tracking links and analytics
readings: Keymailer and Woovit platform documentation
GDC Vault: 'Working with Streamers: A Developer's Guide'
Case study: how Valheim generated 10 million sales primarily through streamer coverage

lecture_notes.introduction: Influencer marketing has become the most effective customer acquisition channel for games, surpassing traditional press coverage in both reach and conversion. A single video from the right YouTuber or a few hours of Twitch coverage from a popular streamer can generate more wishlists than months of social media posting. But influencer relations is not simply sending keys to everyone with a large follower count. It is a strategic discipline that requires careful creator research, personalized outreach, and respectful professional relationships. The most common mistake developers make is treating influencers as a broadcast channel rather than as creative professionals with their own audiences, preferences, and editorial standards. This session covers the complete influencer relations pipeline: how to identify the right creators, how to approach them professionally, how to manage embargo timing and exclusive access, and how to measure results. We will also discuss the ethics of influencer marketing and the legal requirements around disclosure.
key_concepts: Creator identification and tiering: mega (1M+), macro (100K-1M), mid-tier (10K-100K), and micro (1K-10K) creators each serve different strategic purposes and have different response rates
Audience alignment over follower count: a 20K subscriber channel focused on your exact genre is more valuable than a 500K channel that covers everything

Outreach email anatomy: personalized opening referencing specific content, concise game description with unique hook, clear offer (key, early access, exclusive), press kit link, and professional sign-off
Key distribution strategy: stagger keys by creator tier, use platforms like Keymailer for verification, track which keys are redeemed and which generate coverage
Embargo management: coordinating preview and review embargo dates to create a wave of coverage that peaks at launch
Exclusive access as currency: first-look videos, exclusive reveals, and early access builds create incentives for high-priority creators to cover your game
Twitch vs. YouTube strategy: Twitch drives immediate wishlists during live streams but content disappears, YouTube creates evergreen content that drives wishlists for months
Measuring influencer ROI: UTM tracking links, Steam wishlist spikes correlated with coverage dates, and conversion tracking through unique discount codes
Legal requirements: FTC guidelines require disclosure of free keys and sponsored content, and violations carry real consequences for both creator and developer
Building long-term creator relationships: treating influencers as partners rather than one-time promotional channels creates advocates who cover your future games
lecture_notes.demonstration: Outreach simulation: students build a list of 15 content creators for a sample game, tiered by priority. They write personalized outreach emails for three creators of different sizes, craft an embargo timeline, and set up UTM tracking links. The class then role-plays as creators receiving pitches, evaluating which outreach emails would generate a response and why.
setup_requirements: YouTube and Twitch browsing for creator research
Keymailer developer account (free trial)
Email template system (Google Docs or Notion)
UTM link builder (Google Campaign URL Builder)

review.value: Generic mass emails have a response rate under 2%. Personalized emails referencing specific videos have a response rate of 15-25%. The time investment in personalization pays for itself.
Mid-tier creators (10K-100K) often provide the best ROI: they are responsive to outreach, their audiences are engaged, and they genuinely enjoy discovering new games.
Previews lift embargo 5-7 days before launch to build awareness. Reviews lift embargo 1-3 days before launch to drive day-one purchases. Staggering creates sustained coverage.
Every key sent should be trackable. Every link shared should have UTM parameters. Without measurement, influencer marketing is guesswork. Correlate coverage dates with wishlist spikes.

class_schedule.topic: Platform Requirements: Steam, Consoles, and Mobile
learning_objectives: Navigate Steam's store page setup, tagging system, and discoverability algorithms
Understand console certification requirements and their impact on marketing timelines
Compare app store optimization strategies for iOS and Android
Optimize store page elements (descriptions, tags, screenshots) for conversion
readings: Steamworks documentation: Store Page Best Practices
Chris Zukowski, 'How the Steam Algorithm Really Works' deep dive
App Annie/Sensor Tower mobile store optimization guides

lecture_notes.introduction: Every platform has its own rules, algorithms, and audience expectations for game discovery and purchase. Steam operates fundamentally differently from the PlayStation Store, which operates differently from the App Store. Understanding these platform mechanics is not optional: they directly determine how many people see your game and how effectively you convert browsers into buyers. This session provides a deep dive into platform-specific requirements and optimization strategies, with primary focus on Steam as the dominant PC platform. We will examine how Steam's discovery algorithm works, what factors influence visibility, and how to optimize every element of your store page for maximum conversion. We will also cover the certification and submission processes for PlayStation, Xbox, and Nintendo Switch, and the app store optimization techniques that drive mobile game discovery. The goal is practical platform literacy: knowing what each platform requires, how to prepare for it, and how to maximize your game's visibility within each ecosystem.
key_concepts: Steam store page anatomy: header capsule, screenshots, trailer, short and long descriptions, tags, system requirements, and the critical 'above the fold' content that appears without scrolling
Steam discovery algorithm: how the algorithm uses tags, wishlists, user behavior, and engagement metrics to recommend games, and how the new release visibility boost works
Steam tags and categories: selecting the right combination of genre tags and user-defined tags to place your game in front of the right audience segments
Store page conversion optimization: A/B testing screenshots, refining descriptions based on bounce rates, and using the 'about this game' section for SEO
Console certification process: platform-specific requirements for PlayStation, Xbox, and Nintendo that add weeks to your timeline and require advance planning
Console marketing support: platform holder promotional opportunities, featuring on digital storefronts, and the business development relationship
Mobile app store optimization (ASO): keyword research, icon design, screenshot sequences, preview videos, and the importance of ratings and reviews
Cross-platform launch strategy: simultaneous vs. staggered launch, platform exclusivity deals, and managing different audience expectations
Platform-specific pricing: regional pricing matrices, launch discount strategy, and how different platforms handle sales events
lecture_notes.demonstration: Steam store page audit: we analyze five live Steam store pages (two well-optimized, two poorly-optimized, one average) using a scoring rubric that evaluates capsule image, screenshot quality and order, description effectiveness, tag selection, and trailer placement. Students identify specific improvements for each page and predict which would have higher conversion rates, then we check actual review volumes as a proxy for commercial performance.
setup_requirements: Steamworks partner documentation access
Steam store browsing for live page analysis
Store page audit rubric (provided on LMS)
Mobile device for app store browsing

review.value: Steam recommends games based on tag matching, wishlist behavior, and engagement metrics. New releases get a visibility boost that decays over 2-3 weeks. Front-loading wishlists maximizes this boost.
The first two screenshots are seen by every visitor. They should show your game's most impressive and unique moments. Do not start with a title screen or menu.
Budget 4-8 weeks for first-party certification. Failures require resubmission and restart the clock. Platform-specific technical requirements must be built into the development timeline.
App store search drives 65% of mobile game installs. Keyword research and optimization in title, subtitle, and keyword fields has direct, measurable impact on organic discovery.

learning_objectives: Evaluate the trade-offs between signing with a publisher and self-publishing
Analyze standard publishing contract terms including revenue splits, IP ownership, and milestone structures
Identify what different publisher tiers offer and what they expect in return
Prepare a pitch deck and demo that attracts publisher interest
readings: Jason Della Rocca, 'The Publisher Landscape for Indie Developers'
GDC Vault: 'A Practical Guide to Game Publishing Contracts'
Game Developer Magazine: 'What Publishers Actually Do (And What They Don't)'

lecture_notes.introduction: The decision to sign with a publisher or self-publish is one of the most consequential choices a game developer will make. A good publishing deal provides funding, marketing expertise, platform relationships, and QA support that can elevate a game far beyond what a developer could achieve alone. A bad publishing deal can lock a developer into unfavorable revenue splits, strip them of IP ownership, impose unrealistic milestones, and leave them with less money and less control than self-publishing would have provided. This session examines the publishing landscape in detail, covering what publishers of different sizes offer, what they expect in return, and how to evaluate whether a deal is fair. We will analyze real contract terms, discuss negotiation strategies, and examine case studies of both successful partnerships and disastrous deals. The goal is not to advocate for or against publishing, but to give you the knowledge to make an informed decision and negotiate from a position of understanding.
key_concepts: Publisher tiers: AAA publishers (EA, Ubisoft), AA/mid-tier (Focus, THQ Nordic), indie publishers (Devolver, Annapurna, Raw Fury), and micro-publishers (niche, genre-focused)
What publishers provide: funding (development advances), marketing and PR, platform relationships, localization, QA, and business operations support
Revenue split models: standard 70/30 developer/publisher after recoup, but terms vary widely. Understand gross vs. net revenue, recoupment mechanics, and platform fees
IP ownership: the most critical contract term. Retaining IP gives the developer control over sequels, adaptations, and merchandise. Losing IP means losing the franchise
Milestone-based funding: development is funded in tranches tied to milestone deliverables. Understand approval processes, milestone definitions, and what happens when milestones are missed
Rights reversion clauses: what happens to the publishing deal after a set period. Ensure rights revert to the developer if the publisher stops actively selling the game
The publisher pitch: what publishers look for in a submission (unique hook, market positioning, team track record, vertical slice, financial projections)
Self-publishing advantages: 100% creative control, higher per-unit revenue, direct player relationship, and faster decision-making
Self-publishing challenges: marketing expertise gap, platform relationship building, upfront capital requirements, and the time cost of business operations
Hybrid models: working with a marketing agency or PR firm while self-publishing, or using a publisher for specific territories while self-publishing in others
lecture_notes.demonstration: Contract analysis exercise: students receive a redacted but realistic indie publishing contract and identify the key terms: revenue split, recoupment terms, IP ownership, milestone structure, rights reversion timeline, and termination clauses. The class then negotiates improvements to unfavorable terms, with the instructor playing the role of publisher representative.
setup_requirements: Sample publishing contract (redacted, provided on LMS)
Publisher comparison spreadsheet template
Pitch deck template (Google Slides or PowerPoint)
List of active indie publishers with submission guidelines

review.value: Never sign away IP ownership unless the compensation is extraordinary. Your IP is the long-term asset. A game can generate revenue for decades through sequels, ports, and adaptations.
A 70/30 split sounds developer-friendly until you account for platform fees (30%), recoupment of the advance, and net vs. gross accounting. Model the actual numbers before signing.
Ensure the contract includes a rights reversion clause: if the publisher stops actively marketing or selling the game, full rights revert to the developer after a defined period.
Self-publishing is viable when the developer has marketing skills or can hire them, has sufficient capital, and the game's audience can be reached without platform holder relationships.

learning_objectives: Design a Kickstarter or crowdfunding campaign with realistic funding goals and reward tiers
Build pre-launch audience and email lists to ensure day-one campaign momentum
Create campaign page content including video, descriptions, and stretch goals
Manage backer communication and fulfillment logistics
readings: Thomas Bidaux, 'Crowdfunding for Games' annual report from ICO Partners
Kickstarter Creator Handbook (games category)
Post-mortems: Shovel Knight, Bloodstained, and Eiyuden Chronicle campaigns

lecture_notes.introduction: Crowdfunding has funded some of the most beloved indie games of the past decade, from Shovel Knight to Hollow Knight to Divinity: Original Sin. But the crowdfunding landscape has matured significantly since its early days, and the strategies that worked in 2014 do not work today. Modern crowdfunding success requires an existing audience, professional campaign materials, realistic financial planning, and a commitment to years of backer communication. This session covers the complete crowdfunding pipeline: pre-launch audience building, campaign page design, reward tier strategy, funding goal calculation, and post-campaign fulfillment. We will examine campaigns that succeeded and failed, identifying the specific factors that determined their outcomes. The central lesson is that crowdfunding is not a discovery mechanism; it is a conversion mechanism. You must bring the audience to the campaign page. The campaign itself converts existing interest into funding.
key_concepts: Pre-launch audience requirement: successful campaigns have 500-2,000 email subscribers or equivalent engaged followers before launch, because 30-40% of total funding typically comes in the first 48 hours
Funding goal calculation: production costs plus platform fees (5%) plus payment processing (3-5%) plus fulfillment costs plus a 15-20% contingency buffer for unexpected expenses
Reward tier design: digital-only tiers are high-margin, physical rewards (art books, figurines) are costly and complex. Most funding comes from the $20-50 tier range
Campaign page structure: video (2-3 minutes), game description with screenshots and GIFs, team credentials, development timeline, reward tiers, stretch goals, and risk disclosure
Stretch goals: planned extensions that create ongoing excitement and sharing incentives. Must be achievable without derailing the core project scope
Campaign video production: must communicate the game's appeal, the team's credibility, and the campaign's value proposition within the first 30 seconds
Day-one momentum: Kickstarter's algorithm promotes campaigns that fund quickly. Activating your existing audience on day one creates algorithmic amplification
Backer communication: regular, honest updates throughout development maintain trust. Silence erodes confidence and generates negative word-of-mouth
Fulfillment logistics: physical reward production, international shipping costs, customs duties, and the timeline from campaign end to delivery can be a project in itself
lecture_notes.demonstration: Campaign page workshop: students design a complete Kickstarter campaign page for a hypothetical game project. They calculate a funding goal from a realistic budget, design five reward tiers, write the campaign description, plan stretch goals, and outline a communication schedule. The class then evaluates each campaign page as potential backers, identifying what would make them pledge or pass.
setup_requirements: Kickstarter account for browsing campaign examples
Budget spreadsheet template for funding goal calculation
Campaign page wireframe template
Video script template for campaign video planning

review.value: Crowdfunding does not find your audience; it monetizes an audience you have already built. The months of community building before launch are more important than the campaign itself.
30-40% of total funding arrives in the first 48 hours. If you do not hit 30% of your goal in this window, the campaign is unlikely to succeed. Pre-launch email activation is critical.
Every stretch goal adds scope and delays delivery. Design stretch goals that enhance the core game without fundamentally expanding it. Reject feature creep disguised as generosity.
Physical rewards attract pledges but eat margins. A $50 tier with a $15 art book costs $8-12 to print and $5-15 to ship internationally. Calculate fulfillment costs before offering physical goods.

learning_objectives: Create a comprehensive launch plan with milestones from T-90 to T+30
Maximize wishlist accumulation through strategic timing and cross-promotion
Coordinate marketing activities across press, influencers, social media, and community for launch day
Prepare contingency plans for technical issues, negative reviews, and underperformance
readings: Chris Zukowski, 'The Complete Guide to Steam Wishlists'
Steamworks: Launch Day Best Practices documentation
Post-mortem launch analyses from Gamasutra/Game Developer

lecture_notes.introduction: Launch day is the culmination of months or years of development and marketing work, compressed into a single high-stakes moment. The decisions you make in the 90 days before launch and the 30 days after have more impact on your game's commercial trajectory than any other period. A well-executed launch creates a self-reinforcing cycle: strong first-day sales trigger algorithmic promotion, which drives more sales, which generates more reviews, which builds social proof that sustains momentum. A botched launch, whether from technical issues, poor timing, or insufficient marketing preparation, is extremely difficult to recover from. This session covers launch planning in granular detail: the specific milestones, marketing activities, and operational preparations required for a successful launch. We will build a complete launch timeline and discuss the decisions that must be made at each point, including the contingency plans you hope you never need.
key_concepts: Wishlist accumulation strategy: the Coming Soon page should be live 6-12 months before launch to build wishlists through discovery queue, curators, and organic search
Wishlist conversion benchmarks: 15-20% of wishlists convert to purchases on launch day, 40-60% over the first year. A game needs 7,000+ wishlists at launch for a strong first week

Launch timeline milestones: T-90 (finalize marketing materials), T-60 (begin influencer outreach), T-30 (embargo keys distributed), T-14 (social media campaign ramp), T-7 (preview embargo lifts), T-3 (review embargo lifts), T-0 (launch), T+7 (first patch and metrics review)
Release date selection: avoid major AAA launches, consider seasonal patterns (Q1 and Q3 tend to be less competitive), align with Steam seasonal sales strategy
Launch day operations: coordinated announcements across all channels, community team actively monitoring for bugs and feedback, dev team on standby for hotfixes
Launch discount strategy: a 10-15% launch week discount drives impulse purchases and appears in Steam's special offers section, but impacts per-unit revenue
Review management: encouraging reviews without incentivizing them, responding to negative reviews professionally, and understanding how review ratio affects the Steam algorithm
Post-launch momentum: the first major update within 2-4 weeks signals ongoing support and generates a second wave of coverage and sales
Contingency planning: preparing for server overload, game-breaking bugs on launch day, review bombing, and sales significantly below or above projections
lecture_notes.demonstration: Launch plan construction: the class collaboratively builds a complete launch timeline for a sample game on a whiteboard, from T-90 through T+30. Each student contributes a milestone with its associated marketing actions, dependencies, and contingencies. We then stress-test the plan by introducing scenarios: 'What if the build fails certification at T-7?' 'What if a major competitor announces a surprise launch the same week?' The exercise produces a template students can adapt for their own projects.
setup_requirements: Launch timeline template (Gantt chart format)
Steamworks launch checklist documentation
Sample wishlist data for conversion modeling
Whiteboard or collaborative document for group planning

review.value: Aim for 7,000+ wishlists at launch. Below 2,000, consider delaying launch to build more. Wishlists are the single best predictor of first-week sales on Steam.
A 10-15% launch discount drives urgency and places your game in the Specials section. Most successful indie launches use this strategy despite the per-unit revenue impact.
Ship a meaningful content update within 2-4 weeks of launch. This generates a second wave of coverage, demonstrates ongoing support, and brings back players who bounced.

Plan for three scenarios: sales 50% below projections (reduce marketing spend, focus on patches), sales on target (execute planned marketing), and sales 3x above projections (prepare server scaling, accelerate content pipeline).

learning_objectives: Develop a post-launch content roadmap that sustains player engagement and revenue
Design DLC and expansion strategies that add value without fragmenting the player base
Manage community expectations during the transition from active development to maintenance
Plan seasonal sales participation and long-tail revenue optimization
readings: Steamworks: Post-Launch Best Practices and Visibility Rounds
GDC Vault: 'The Long Tail: Sustaining Indie Games After Launch'
Case studies: Stardew Valley, Hollow Knight, and Dead Cells post-launch strategies

lecture_notes.introduction: Launch is not the finish line; it is the starting line. The majority of a game's lifetime revenue comes after the first week, driven by sales events, word-of-mouth, content updates, and DLC. Games that receive ongoing support after launch build stronger communities, generate more positive reviews, and achieve significantly higher lifetime revenue than games that launch and are abandoned. This session covers post-launch strategy: how to plan a content roadmap, when and how to release DLC, how to participate in seasonal sales events, and how to manage the long transition from active development to maintenance mode. We will examine games that achieved remarkable long-tail success through sustained post-launch support and contrast them with games that squandered strong launches by failing to follow through. The strategic question is not whether to support your game after launch but how to do so sustainably without burning out your team or over-committing resources.
key_concepts: Post-launch content roadmap: planned updates at regular intervals (monthly patches, quarterly content drops) that maintain player engagement and generate press coverage
Steam visibility rounds: each game earns visibility boosts for major updates, encouraging developers to ship meaningful content updates that re-enter the discovery queue
DLC pricing and scope: expansion DLC ($10-20, 10+ hours of content) vs. content packs ($5-10, cosmetics or additional levels) vs. free updates (goodwill and review improvement)
DLC timing: first paid DLC typically 3-6 months after launch, after the base game has been patched and polished. Too early feels like cut content; too late misses the engagement window
Seasonal sales strategy: participating in Steam's four major sales events (Winter, Spring, Summer, Autumn) with appropriate discount depths that increase over time
Discount curve planning: 20% at first sale, 33% at second, 50% after one year, deeper discounts for older titles. Never discount below a price that devalues the product
Community communication during post-launch: regular development updates, transparent roadmaps, and honest communication about priorities and timelines
Long-tail revenue optimization: SEO for store pages, maintaining screenshot and description freshness, and leveraging user-generated content
Knowing when to stop: recognizing diminishing returns on updates and transitioning team resources to the next project without abandoning the existing community
lecture_notes.demonstration: Post-launch roadmap exercise: students create a 12-month post-launch plan for a sample game that just launched to moderate success (5,000 first-week sales, 82% positive reviews). The plan includes a patch schedule, two DLC releases, four seasonal sale participations, community events, and a transition plan. Students calculate projected revenue for each phase using historical conversion data and present their plans for peer evaluation.
setup_requirements: Steamworks update and DLC documentation
Revenue projection spreadsheet template
Post-launch roadmap template (timeline format)
Sample game sales data for modeling

review.value: Each major update earns a Steam visibility round, putting your game back in front of players who wishlisted but did not buy, and new players discovering through the queue.
First paid DLC at 3-6 months post-launch. Free updates between launch and first DLC build goodwill and reviews. DLC after 12+ months targets only the most dedicated fans.
Discounts should increase over time: 20% at 3 months, 33% at 6 months, 50% at 12 months. Each deeper discount reaches a new price-sensitive audience tier.
Plan the transition from active development to maintenance from day one. Communicate the roadmap's end date honestly. Players respect transparency more than promises of infinite support.

learning_objectives: Identify and submit to game festivals and award shows that provide meaningful exposure
Prepare demo builds and booth presentations for in-person and virtual events
Leverage award nominations and wins as marketing assets across all channels
Network effectively at industry events to build relationships with press, publishers, and peers
readings: Complete guide to indie game festivals and submission timelines (curated list on LMS)
GDC Vault: 'Making the Most of Game Festivals as an Indie'
Post-mortems from IGF, IndieCade, and Day of the Devs participants

lecture_notes.introduction: Game festivals, awards, and industry events serve a dual purpose in your marketing strategy: they provide direct audience exposure and they generate marketing assets that amplify all other marketing efforts. An 'Official Selection' laurel on your trailer, a 'Best Indie Game' nomination in your Steam description, or a photograph of a packed booth at PAX creates social proof that elevates every other marketing touchpoint. These events are also irreplaceable networking opportunities where developers meet press, publishers, platform representatives, and fellow developers who become collaborators, mentors, and advocates. This session covers the festival and event landscape: which events to prioritize based on your game's genre and development stage, how to prepare submissions and booth presentations, and how to maximize the marketing value of every event you participate in. We will discuss both physical events like PAX, GDC, and gamescom and digital showcases like the Steam Next Fest, Wholesome Direct, and publisher-organized showcases.
key_concepts: Festival tier system: major (GDC/IGF, PAX, gamescom), mid-tier (IndieCade, BitSummit, Day of the Devs), digital (Steam Next Fest, genre-specific showcases), and regional events
Submission strategy: submit to 10-15 events per year, targeting a mix of tiers. Submission fees range from free to $100. Budget time for build preparation and submission materials
Steam Next Fest: the single most impactful event for indie games on Steam. A well-prepared demo with streaming integration can generate thousands of wishlists in one week
Demo build preparation: the event demo must be a curated experience, not a truncated version of the full game. 15-20 minutes of polished content with a clear ending and a wishlist call-to-action
Booth presentation (physical events): playable demo on multiple stations, vertical banner, business cards, branded swag, and a team member trained to pitch the game in 30 seconds
Award laurels as marketing assets: 'Official Selection' and 'Winner' laurels added to trailers, store pages, and social media create powerful social proof
Networking at events: approach conversations with genuine curiosity, follow up within 48 hours, and focus on building relationships rather than pitching your game
Digital showcase participation: curated showcases (Wholesome Direct, Guerrilla Collective) reach large audiences. Preparing a 60-90 second trailer specifically formatted for showcase requirements
Measuring event ROI: track wishlist spikes, demo downloads, social media follower growth, and press coverage generated by each event to inform future participation decisions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Steam Next Fest preparation workshop: students prepare a complete Next Fest strategy for a sample game. They plan the demo scope and content, create a streaming-friendly overlay, write the event-specific store page copy, plan a social media campaign to drive traffic during the fest, and design a post-demo survey to collect player feedback. The class reviews successful and unsuccessful Next Fest case studies and identifies the specific preparation differences.
setup_requirements: Festival submission calendar template
Steam Next Fest documentation and guidelines
Demo build checklist template
Networking follow-up email templates

review.value: Steam Next Fest is the highest-ROI event for indie games. A well-prepared demo with streaming integration can generate 5,000-20,000 wishlists in a single week. Participate if eligible.
The demo is marketing content, not a product sample. Curate 15-20 minutes of your best content with a deliberate hook, climax, and ending that drives the player to wishlist.
Even a nomination creates a marketing asset. Add laurels to trailers, store pages, social media bios, and press kits. They signal quality validation from industry peers.
Track wishlist spikes correlated with each event. Not all events deliver equal value. After one year, you should know which 3-5 events consistently drive results for your type of game.

learning_objectives: Present a comprehensive marketing plan to a panel simulating industry professionals
Defend marketing strategy decisions with data and competitive analysis
Provide constructive peer feedback on marketing plans using professional evaluation criteria
Synthesize all course concepts into an actionable, realistic marketing strategy
readings: Review all course materials and lecture notes
Prepare final marketing plan presentation (30 minutes including Q&A)
Peer evaluation rubric (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session is the culmination of the entire course. Each student presents a comprehensive marketing plan for a game project, demonstrating mastery of every concept covered: market analysis, audience targeting, branding, press materials, trailers, social media strategy, community building, influencer relations, platform optimization, publishing strategy, launch planning, post-launch support, and event participation. The presentation format simulates a professional pitch to a publisher's marketing team or an investor evaluating commercial viability. You will present your plan in 20 minutes, followed by 10 minutes of questions from the evaluation panel and your peers. The evaluation is not just about the quality of your plan but about your ability to defend your decisions with data, adapt to challenges posed in questions, and demonstrate strategic thinking under pressure. This is the closest simulation we can provide to the real-world experience of pitching and defending a marketing strategy.
key_concepts: Marketing plan structure: executive summary, market analysis, target audience, positioning, brand guidelines, marketing asset inventory, channel strategy, influencer plan, launch timeline, post-launch roadmap, budget, and KPIs
Presentation skills for marketing pitches: leading with the hook, supporting with data, telling a story about the audience journey from awareness to purchase
Budget allocation: distributing a realistic marketing budget across channels based on expected ROI, with justification for each allocation decision
KPI definition: setting measurable targets for each phase (wishlists at Coming Soon, social media growth rate, review targets, first-week sales, month-one retention)
Risk assessment: identifying the three biggest risks to commercial success and articulating mitigation strategies for each
Peer evaluation: developing critical analysis skills by evaluating classmates' plans using a professional rubric
Adaptability under questioning: demonstrating depth of understanding by fielding challenging questions about strategy assumptions and alternative approaches
Portfolio integration: this marketing plan becomes a portfolio piece demonstrating strategic thinking to potential employers in game marketing and production
lecture_notes.demonstration: The instructor presents a sample marketing plan presentation first, modeling the expected format, depth, and presentation style. The plan is deliberately imperfect, and the class identifies areas for improvement before beginning their own presentations. This establishes the evaluation standard and helps calibrate peer feedback.
setup_requirements: Presentation software (PowerPoint, Google Slides, or Keynote)
Projector or screen sharing for presentations
Peer evaluation forms (printed or digital)
Timer for 20-minute presentation windows

review.value: A complete marketing plan covers every phase from pre-announcement through one year post-launch, with specific actions, timelines, and measurable targets at each stage.
Every strategic choice should be supported by market data, competitor analysis, or audience research. Unsupported assertions weaken the plan's credibility.
The budget must be realistic for the game's scale. An indie game with a $0 marketing budget uses different strategies than one with $50K. Both can succeed but require different approaches.
This marketing plan is a demonstrable portfolio piece. Refine it after the course and include it in your professional portfolio as evidence of strategic marketing capability.

quizzes.topic: Market Analysis, Audience Targeting, and Branding

questions.question: A student is developing a pixel-art Metroidvania. Using the Boxleiter method and Steam tag data, outline a competitive analysis for this game. Identify at least 4 specific competitors, estimate their revenue, and assess whether the market has room for a new entry. Explain your methodology.
questions.answer: The student should identify 4+ specific pixel-art Metroidvania games (e.g., Hollow Knight, Axiom Verge, Blasphemous, Dead Cells), pull their review counts from SteamDB, apply the Boxleiter multiplier (reviews x 20-60 depending on genre), and calculate estimated revenue. They should then assess tag saturation by comparing the number of games tagged 'Metroidvania' against cumulative review volume. A strong answer concludes with a market assessment noting that while the genre is competitive, consistent demand exists as evidenced by strong sales for recent entries, and differentiates based on the student's unique angle.

rubric.key: Identifies 4+ specific competitors with review data
Correctly applies Boxleiter method with appropriate multiplier
Assesses market saturation using tag data
Provides actionable market entry conclusion

questions.question: Write a complete positioning statement for a cozy farming simulation game with a unique twist: the farm exists on a space station orbiting Earth. Include primary and secondary audience definitions and explain how your positioning would influence trailer editing, influencer selection, and community platform choice.
questions.answer: Positioning statement should follow the formula: 'For fans of relaxing life sims who want a fresh setting, [Game Title] is a cozy farming game set on a space station that delivers the satisfying loop of Stardew Valley with the wonder of outer space exploration, unlike traditional farming sims which are all grounded on Earth.' Primary audience: cozy game enthusiasts aged 18-35 who play Stardew Valley, My Time at Portia, and Spiritfarer. Secondary audience: casual sci-fi fans and space game players looking for lower-stakes gameplay. Trailer implications: warm color palette despite space setting, show crop growth loops and character interactions before sci-fi elements. Influencer targets: cozy game YouTubers like Dani, The Librarian, and similar. Community: Discord with cozy-themed channels, r/cozy_gaming and r/indiegaming subreddits.

rubric.key: Positioning statement follows formula with clear differentiation
Primary and secondary audiences are distinct and data-informed
Correctly traces positioning implications to trailer, influencers, and community

questions.question: A game developer shows you their Steam capsule image: it features their game's logo in a stylized fantasy font over a dark, detailed illustration of a castle. The logo is barely readable at thumbnail size and the castle blends into a sea of similar fantasy game capsules. Identify three specific branding problems and propose concrete fixes for each.
questions.answer: Problem 1: Logo illegibility at thumbnail size. Fix: simplify the logo, increase contrast between logo and background, add a subtle glow or drop shadow, and test at 231x87 pixels. Problem 2: Visual indistinction from competitors. Fix: use a unique color palette that breaks from dark fantasy conventions (perhaps saturated jewel tones or a distinctive color accent), or feature the player character prominently instead of environment. Problem 3: Castle image does not communicate gameplay. Fix: replace or overlay with a scene showing the core gameplay mechanic in action, with the player character visible and the genre immediately identifiable.

rubric.key: Identifies logo readability issue with specific fix
Identifies visual distinction issue with specific fix
Identifies gameplay communication issue with specific fix

questions.question: You are reviewing an indie developer's press kit before their game launch. The press kit contains: 3 screenshots (all of menu screens), a 500-word description that lists every feature without a hook sentence, a logo in JPG format only, and no trailer. Write a prioritized improvement plan with at least 6 specific action items, explaining why each matters.
questions.answer: Priority 1: Replace menu screenshots with 6-8 gameplay screenshots showing the core loop, unique mechanics, and best visual moments. Journalists cannot write about a game from menu screenshots. Priority 2: Rewrite the description starting with a hook sentence (genre + unique twist), followed by a core loop paragraph, 3-5 bullet-point USPs, and comparison hooks. The current wall of features overwhelms and fails to communicate what makes the game special. Priority 3: Produce a 60-90 second gameplay trailer as the single most important missing asset. Priority 4: Provide the logo as PNG with transparency and as a vector file (SVG or AI) so press can use it on any background. Priority 5: Add a factsheet with release date, platforms, price, and contact email in scannable format. Priority 6: Create key art in multiple aspect ratios (16:9, 1:1, 9:16) for different platform requirements.

rubric.key: Correctly prioritizes screenshot replacement and describes what to capture
Description rewrite follows hook-loop-USP-comparison formula
Identifies trailer as critical missing asset with specifications
Addresses logo format, factsheet, and key art with specific requirements

questions.question: You have 60 seconds of raw gameplay footage of a fast-paced action roguelike. Storyboard a 45-second trailer. Describe each shot (with approximate duration), the music style, any text overlays, and how you would open and close the trailer. Explain your pacing decisions.
questions.answer: Opening (0-3s): Cold open on the most visually explosive combat moment, mid-action, no logo, no fade-in. Immediately communicates genre and energy level. Music: electronic/synth with driving beat, begins at full energy. Shots 2-4 (3-12s): Quick cuts (2-3s each) showing different weapon types, enemy encounters, and environments. Cuts land on beat drops. Demonstrates gameplay variety. Shot 5 (12-18s): Slightly longer shot of a boss encounter, showing scale and challenge. Music builds tension. Text overlay (18-20s): Game title and tagline (4-6 words). Brief breathing room in music. Shots 6-8 (20-30s): Montage of unique mechanics: power-ups, room transitions, death-and-restart cycle. Fastest cuts in the trailer, 1-2 seconds each. Demonstrates depth. Shot 9 (30-38s): Extended gameplay sequence showing a successful run, building to a climax. Music crescendo. Closing (38-45s): Title card, platforms, release date or 'Wishlist Now', and store logos. Music resolves. Pacing rationale: front-load the hook, alternate between quick montage and breathing room, build to a climax that mirrors the game's own progression loop.

rubric.key: Opens with a hook in the first 3 seconds, not a logo
Pacing varies between quick cuts and breathing room with musical sync
Shot selection demonstrates game variety and unique mechanics
Closes with clear call to action and platform information

questions.question: Explain why a game trailer must work without audio for Steam's autoplay feature. Describe three specific techniques for making the first five seconds of a trailer compelling when muted.
questions.answer: Steam autoplays trailers on hover in the store browse view with audio muted. The first 5 seconds must convince a browser to click and enable sound. Technique 1: Use the most visually dynamic gameplay moment with high contrast, movement, and color to capture attention through motion alone. Technique 2: Add a brief text overlay or animated title card that communicates the game's core hook without requiring audio context. Technique 3: Ensure visual clarity by avoiding dark or visually ambiguous opening shots; the genre and gameplay style should be immediately identifiable from visuals alone, without narration or music cues to guide interpretation.

rubric.key: Correctly explains Steam autoplay muting behavior
Three distinct and practical techniques described
Techniques specifically address visual-only communication

quizzes.topic: Social Media, Community Building, and Influencer Relations

questions.question: A solo indie developer has a puzzle game launching in 4 months and zero social media following. They have 2 hours per week to dedicate to marketing. Design a social media strategy that maximizes impact within these constraints. Specify platform selection (with justification), content types, posting frequency, and realistic growth targets.
questions.answer: Platform: Twitter/X as primary (largest indie game dev community, GIF-friendly for puzzle games, hashtag discovery), TikTok as secondary if the game has visually satisfying solutions. Posting frequency: 3 Twitter posts per week, 1 TikTok per week. Content types: Puzzle solution GIFs (shareable, demonstrate core mechanic), before/after development shots, weekly #ScreenshotSaturday posts, and occasional development insights. Growth targets: realistic for 4 months from zero: 500-1,000 Twitter followers, 2,000-5,000 TikTok followers if puzzle solutions have viral potential. Time allocation: 1 hour batch creating content on weekends, 30 minutes posting and engaging during the week, 30 minutes community engagement in puzzle game subreddits. Key tactic: cross-post puzzle solution clips with 'Can you solve this?' hooks that encourage engagement.

rubric.key: Platform selection justified by audience behavior and time constraints
Content types are specific and suited to puzzle game genre
Posting frequency is realistic for 2 hours per week
Growth targets are honest and data-informed, not aspirational

questions.question: Write a complete outreach email to a mid-tier YouTube creator (75,000 subscribers) who primarily reviews puzzle and strategy games. Your game is a minimalist puzzle game where players manipulate gravity to guide light through crystal mazes. Include every element of an effective outreach email.
questions.answer: Subject: Luminous Maze - gravity-bending puzzles for your channel. Body: Open with personalized reference to a specific recent video (e.g., 'I loved your analysis of The Witness's environmental storytelling in last week's video'). Introduce the game concisely: 'Luminous Maze is a minimalist puzzle game where you rotate gravity to guide beams of light through crystal mazes, creating cascading refractions that solve each level.' Comparison hooks: 'Fans of The Witness and Baba Is You have been the most engaged in our beta.' Clear offer: 'I would love to send you a Steam key for an early look. We are also happy to provide exclusive access to our new crystal garden biome if you are interested in a first-look video.' Include: direct press kit link, trailer link, and a note about the game's release date and platform. Professional sign-off with full name, title, and contact information.

rubric.key: Personalized opening referencing specific creator content
Game description is concise with unique hook and comparison titles
Clear offer with press kit and trailer links included
Professional tone with proper sign-off and contact details

questions.question: Your game's Discord server has 2,000 members, but daily active participation has dropped from 150 messages per day to 30 over the past month. The game is 3 months from launch. Diagnose three possible causes for the engagement decline and propose a specific intervention for each.
questions.answer: Cause 1: Content drought. If development updates have slowed, members have nothing new to discuss.
