Intervention: institute weekly development diary posts with screenshots, even small ones, and ask specific questions that invite responses ('Which of these two color palettes do you prefer for the forest biome?'). Cause 2: Toxic or unwelcoming atmosphere. A few dominant negative voices can drive away the majority. Intervention: review recent message history for toxicity, enforce moderation rules, recruit 2-3 active positive members as volunteer moderators, and create a 'community guidelines' pinned post. Cause 3: Lack of interactive activities. A server with only announcement and general channels becomes stale. Intervention: add weekly community events (screenshot contests, design challenges, Q&A sessions with the developer), create themed channels that encourage creative participation, and implement a role-based reward system for active contributors.

rubric.key: Three distinct and plausible causes identified
Each intervention is specific and actionable, not generic
Interventions address root causes rather than symptoms

quizzes.topic: Platform Strategy, Publishing Deals, and Crowdfunding

questions.question: A developer has received a publishing offer with the following terms: 60/40 revenue split (developer/publisher) after recoupment of a $200K advance, publisher retains IP ownership, 5-year exclusivity period with no rights reversion clause. The developer's game has 15,000 Steam wishlists and a strong demo reception. Evaluate this deal: identify the three most concerning terms, explain why each is problematic, and suggest what the developer should negotiate for.
questions.answer: Concerning term 1: IP ownership by publisher. This is the most critical issue. The developer loses control over sequels, adaptations, and the franchise long-term. Negotiate: retain IP ownership, grant the publisher a license for this specific title only. Concerning term 2: No rights reversion clause. If the publisher stops marketing or selling the game, the developer is locked in for 5 years with no recourse. Negotiate: include a clause that reverts all rights to the developer if annual revenue falls below a threshold (e.g., $10K) for two consecutive years, or after the exclusivity period ends. Concerning term 3: 60/40 split is below the industry standard of 70/30. With 15K wishlists and strong demo reception, the developer has leverage. Negotiate: 70/30 after recoupment, or a sliding scale that increases the developer's share as cumulative revenue crosses milestones. Additional note: with 15K wishlists, the developer may be viable for self-publishing, which should be considered as a BATNA in negotiations.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies IP ownership as the most critical concern with specific negotiation counter
Identifies rights reversion absence with specific clause recommendation
Evaluates revenue split against industry standards with negotiation leverage analysis
Considers self-publishing as alternative given wishlist count

questions.question: Design a Kickstarter reward tier structure for a $50,000 campaign for a narrative adventure game. Include at least 5 tiers with prices, descriptions, and estimated fulfillment costs. Calculate the effective margin for each tier and explain your pricing strategy.
questions.answer: Tier 1 - $5 'Supporter': name in credits. Cost: $0. Margin: ~$4.25 after platform/processing fees. Purpose: low barrier entry, boosts backer count. Tier 2 - $20 'Digital Edition': game key + digital soundtrack + digital art book. Cost: $0 marginal. Margin: ~$17. Purpose: primary revenue tier, expected to capture 60% of backers. Tier 3 - $40 'Collector's Digital': game key + soundtrack + art book + beta access + behind-the-scenes documentary. Cost: documentary production amortized. Margin: ~$34. Purpose: premium digital tier. Tier 4 - $75 'Physical Edition': all digital rewards + physical art book + enamel pin. Cost: ~$20 (printing $12, pin $3, packaging/shipping $5 domestic). Margin: ~$43 domestic, ~$30 international. Purpose: physical collectors. Tier 5 - $150 'Design Your NPC': all physical rewards + design an NPC that appears in the game. Cost: ~$25 (physical) + development time. Margin: ~$102. Limited to 20 backers. Purpose: high-value exclusive. Strategy: anchor most backers at $20 tier where margins are highest, use physical tiers to attract attention but limit quantities to control fulfillment costs.

rubric.key: 5+ tiers with realistic prices and clear value progression
Fulfillment costs estimated for physical tiers including shipping
Margin calculations account for platform and processing fees
Strategic rationale for tier structure explains expected backer distribution

questions.question: Explain the concept of Steam's 'new release visibility boost' and describe three specific marketing actions a developer should take in the two weeks before launch to maximize the value of this boost.
questions.answer: Steam gives every new release a visibility boost in the Discovery Queue, New Releases tab, and tag-based recommendation algorithm for approximately 2-3 weeks after launch. The boost's effectiveness depends on how well the game converts visibility into sales during this window. Action 1: Ensure maximum wishlists before launch by activating email lists, social media audiences, and community channels in the final two weeks. Wishlists convert to day-one purchases which signal strong demand to the algorithm. Action 2: Coordinate influencer and press coverage to peak during launch week, creating external traffic that compounds the algorithmic boost. Multiple coverage sources hitting simultaneously create a perception of momentum. Action 3: Optimize the store page for conversion: finalize screenshot order, polish the short description, verify the trailer plays well on autoplay, and ensure tags accurately target the intended audience. The visibility boost sends traffic to your store page; the page's conversion rate determines whether that traffic becomes sales that sustain the boost.

rubric.key: Correctly describes the new release visibility boost mechanism and duration
Wishlist activation strategy with specific channels
Coverage coordination timing aligned with boost window
Store page optimization tied to conversion during boost

questions.question: Your indie game launched one week ago. First-week sales were 3,200 units (you projected 5,000). Reviews are 89% positive (47 reviews). Your Discord has 800 active members who are enthusiastic. Wishlist count at launch was 8,500. Diagnose the underperformance and create a 30-day recovery plan with specific actions and metrics targets.
questions.answer: Diagnosis: With 8,500 wishlists, a 37.6% first-week conversion is actually above the 15-20% benchmark, suggesting wishlists were effectively activated but the total wishlist count was insufficient for the 5,000-unit target. The high review score (89%) and active community indicate the game is good but lacked awareness. The problem was insufficient marketing reach, not conversion. 30-day recovery plan: Week 1-2: Leverage the positive reviews and community enthusiasm. Create an accolades trailer featuring review quotes. Increase influencer outreach to mid-tier creators who missed the launch, offering keys with the social proof of positive reviews. Target: 20 new outreach emails, 5 pieces of new coverage. Week 2-3: Ship a meaningful free content update to earn a Steam visibility round. Announce the update on social media with a 20% discount. Target: 500 additional sales during the update window. Week 3-4: Submit to Steam Next Fest for the next cycle with a polished demo if eligible, or apply to upcoming game festivals. Begin building toward the first seasonal sale. Target: 2,000 additional wishlists. Metric targets: 5,000 total units sold by day 30, 100+ reviews maintaining 85%+ positive, 12,000 total wishlists for long-tail conversion.

rubric.key: Correct diagnosis: awareness problem, not conversion problem, with wishlist math
Recovery plan has specific weekly actions, not vague suggestions
Leverages existing strengths (reviews, community) rather than starting from scratch
Metric targets are realistic and measurable

questions.question: Compare Steam Next Fest and PAX as marketing opportunities for an indie developer with a $2,000 marketing budget. Evaluate cost, reach, effort required, and expected outcomes for each. Which would you recommend for a single-player narrative game and why?
questions.answer: Steam Next Fest: Cost is effectively free (no registration fee, requires a polished demo build). Reach is massive: millions of Steam users browse the fest. Effort is moderate: demo build preparation, store page optimization, and social media promotion during the week. Expected outcome: 2,000-15,000 wishlists depending on game appeal and demo quality. Works entirely online, no travel required. PAX: Cost is high: booth space ($2,000-5,000 for indie), travel, hotel, equipment rental, printed materials. A $2,000 budget barely covers a small booth without travel. Reach is localized to attendees (80,000-100,000 over the event). Effort is very high: demo preparation, booth setup, staffing for 3-4 days. Expected outcome: 200-1,000 wishlists, but also networking, press meetings, and content creator coverage that has indirect value. Recommendation for single-player narrative game: Steam Next Fest is significantly higher ROI. Narrative games benefit from players experiencing the story at their own pace, which a booth environment cannot provide. The demo format lets players discover the game's qualities organically, and the wishlist conversion is direct and measurable.

rubric.key: Accurate cost comparison with specific numbers
Reach and outcome estimates are realistic and differentiated
Effort analysis considers the full scope including preparation
Recommendation is justified with genre-specific reasoning

questions.question: Design a 12-month post-launch DLC and sales strategy for a game that launched at $19.99 with 10,000 first-month sales and 85% positive reviews. Include timing for discounts, content updates, and paid DLC, with projected revenue for each phase.
questions.answer: Month 1-2: Free quality-of-life patch and small content update. Earns Steam visibility round. No discount yet. Projected: 2,000 additional sales at full price ($40K). Month 3: First seasonal sale at 20% off ($15.99). Projected: 3,000 sales ($48K). Month 4-5: Announce and develop first paid DLC ($7.99, 4-6 hours of new content). Builds anticipation. Month 6: DLC release + base game at 33% off ($13.39). Projected: 2,000 DLC sales ($16K) + 2,500 base game sales ($33.5K). Month 9: Second seasonal sale at 40% off ($11.99). Base + DLC bundle at 30% off. Projected: 4,000 base sales ($48K) + 1,500 DLC ($12K). Month 12: Anniversary update (free content), base game at 50% off ($9.99), DLC at 25% off. Projected: 5,000 base sales ($50K) + 2,000 DLC ($12K). Total 12-month projected revenue: approximately $260K gross from base game + DLC, before platform fees.

rubric.key: Discount curve increases appropriately over time
DLC timing allows base game to stabilize first
Revenue projections use realistic conversion assumptions
Free updates are strategically placed to earn visibility rounds

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions and guest speaker presentations. More than 2 unexcused absences result in a half-letter grade reduction. Guest speaker sessions cannot be made up.
course_policies.late_submissions: Marketing is deadline-driven. Late assignments lose 15% per day. No submissions accepted more than 3 days late. Press kit and trailer deadlines are firm because they simulate real launch schedules.
course_policies.collaboration: Students may collaborate on community building exercises and peer review sessions, but all portfolio deliverables (market analysis, press kit, trailer, marketing plan) must be individually produced. Peer review participation contributes to the lab assignments grade.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All marketing copy, visual assets, and strategic analysis must be original work. AI tools may be used for ideation and drafting but final deliverables must reflect the student's own analysis and creative decisions. Use of AI must be disclosed. Presenting others' marketing materials as your own constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: DaVinci Resolve (free) for video editing, Figma or Canva for design, OBS Studio for capture, spreadsheet software for analysis, Discord account for community exercises, and social media accounts on at least two platforms.
